message:
fix(ingest): StreamChannel overflow marker no longer retroactively tags pre-drop buffered events (resolves ft-cw7f1)

Resolves the bead filed in the previous commit
(`ft-cw7f1 — [ingest] StreamChannel EmitGap marks pre-drop buffered
events as overflow`).

**Previous behavior (bug):**

When `StreamChannel::send` dropped an incoming event under `EmitGap`
policy, it also removed the dropped event's pane_id from
`overflow_panes`, with the intent that the *next* received event for
that pane would carry `overflow = true`. In practice, because the
overflow flag was applied eagerly at *send* time on every event that
matched the pane, any event that was already sitting in the buffer
when the drop happened could end up tagged — the drop event's
signal retroactively painted over events that had already been
accepted and committed to the buffer before the gap occurred. The
consumer could not distinguish "data arrived after a gap" from "data
arrived before a gap", which is the whole point of the gap marker.

This is also mirrored on the receive side: the old `recv` path
consulted `overflow_panes` at pop time, which meant an event whose
arrival *predated* any overflow could still pick up the flag on its
way out, just because a later overflow happened before it was
received.

**New behavior (fix):**

Overflow tagging is moved from "eager, applied at send and recv
time" to "structural, applied to the first event after the drop":

- `apply_pending_overflow_to_event(&mut self, event)` handles the
  simple case: if this pane has a pending overflow flag in
  `overflow_panes`, consume it and stamp the incoming event.
- `mark_next_event_for_pane_overflow(&mut self, pane_id, new_event)`
  handles the harder case that previously leaked. When the buffer is
  full and the `DropOldest` branch evicts an event, we need to mark
  *some* event as "this is the first event after the gap". The new
  helper walks the remaining buffer for a same-pane event and marks
  it in place; if none exists (all buffered events are for other
  panes), it falls through to the new event itself; only if neither
  is a same-pane event does it set `overflow_panes[pane_id]` so the
  next *future* send picks it up.
- `recv` reduces to `self.buffer.pop_front()` — the flag is now
  already stamped at structural send-time, so recv does not need to
  mutate state.

**Test updates inside the module:**

The existing `StreamChannel` overflow test is rewritten to assert the
new, correct semantics:

- The two events already buffered at the moment of the drop must NOT
  have `overflow = true`, with explicit assertion messages
  (`"pre-drop buffered events must not be retroactively tagged"` and
  `"all pre-drop buffered events must drain before the gap marker"`)
  so a regression is diagnosable at a glance.
- A new `OutputData` event is sent *after* the drop; the next recv
  must have `overflow = true` because that event is the first
  accepted same-pane event after the gap.

No API surface changes — callers see the same `send -> bool` and
`recv -> Option<StreamEvent>` signatures and the same StreamEvent
enum. Only the timing of when `overflow = true` is set on an event
changes, and it changes to match the documented contract.
